subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Personal Service on Individuals — Methods Under r.16.02
fact_pattern: A process server is attempting to personally serve a statement of claim on Marcus, an individual defendant. Marcus is not at his home but the process server finds Marcus at his place of work. Marcus refuses to accept the documents but the process server leaves them on Marcus's desk in his presence.
call_of_question: Under r.16.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, has personal service on Marcus been validly effected?

options.A: No — personal service on an individual is only valid if the defendant personally accepts the documents from the process server's hands.
options.B: Yes — leaving the documents with Marcus in his presence at his place of work constitutes valid personal service under r.16.02, even though Marcus refused to accept them.
options.C: No — personal service must be effected at the defendant's residence; service at a place of work is not valid personal service.
options.D: No — the process server must first obtain a court order permitting service at the defendant's place of work.

explanation: Under r.16.02(1)(a) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), personal service on an individual shall be made by leaving a copy of the document with the individual. Physical acceptance is not required — leaving the document with the person, whether or not they choose to accept it, constitutes valid personal service. A defendant cannot avoid service by refusing to take documents that are placed in their presence. Personal service may be effected at any location where the individual is found, including a place of work.
why_A_wrong: Rule 16.02(1)(a) does not require the defendant to physically accept the documents. Service is complete when the document is left with (i.e., delivered to) the person; refusal to accept does not invalidate service.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.16.02(1)(a), personal service is effected by leaving the document with the individual; Marcus's refusal does not invalidate the service.
why_C_wrong: Personal service under r.16.02 may be made wherever the individual is found — there is no requirement that service be effected at the defendant's residence.
why_D_wrong: No court order is required to serve an individual at their place of work. Personal service under r.16.02 may be effected wherever the person is located.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Personal Service on Individuals — Methods Under r.16.02
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.02(1)(a)

tested_concepts: personal service on individual — r.16.02(1)(a)
refusal to accept does not invalidate personal service
service may occur at any location

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Personal Service on Corporations — Methods Under r.16.02
fact_pattern: A plaintiff's process server attempts to personally serve a statement of claim on Takahashi Holdings Inc., an Ontario corporation. The process server attends the corporation's registered office and is met by the office receptionist, who says she is authorized to accept service on behalf of the company. The receptionist accepts the documents and signs an acknowledgment.
call_of_question: Under r.16.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, which of the following methods constitutes valid personal service on a corporation?

options.A: Service can only be effected on a corporation by leaving the documents with its registered agent with the court; leaving them with a receptionist is never valid.
options.B: Service on a corporation is effected by leaving a copy of the document with an officer, director, or agent of the corporation, or with a person at any place of business of the corporation who appears to be in control or management of the place of business.
options.C: Service on a corporation must be effected by registered mail to the corporation's head office; personal service is not available against corporations.
options.D: Service on a corporation is only valid if made directly on the CEO or President of the corporation.

explanation: Under r.16.02(1)(c) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), personal service on a corporation may be made by leaving a copy of the document with an officer, director, or agent of the corporation, or with a person at any place of business of the corporation who appears to be in control or management of that place of business. A receptionist who accepts documents on behalf of the company and who appears to be in a position of control at that location qualifies. The process server does not need to find the CEO or a specific named officer.
why_A_wrong: There is no requirement that service be made on a registered agent with the court. Rule 16.02(1)(c) permits service on officers, directors, agents, or persons in control of a place of business.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 16.02(1)(c) permits personal service on a corporation by leaving documents with an officer, director, agent, or a person who appears to be in control of a place of business.
why_C_wrong: Personal service is available against corporations under r.16.02(1)(c). Registered mail is an alternative to personal service under r.16.03, not a requirement.
why_D_wrong: Service does not need to be made on the CEO or President specifically. Rule 16.02(1)(c) permits service on any officer, director, agent, or person in apparent control of a place of business.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Personal Service on Corporations — Methods Under r.16.02
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.02(1)(c)

tested_concepts: personal service on corporation — r.16.02(1)(c)
service on officer director agent or person in control
receptionist as person in control

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Alternative to Personal Service — Methods Under r.16.03
fact_pattern: A process server is unable to personally serve an individual defendant at any location. The plaintiff's lawyer considers using an alternative to personal service permitted by the Rules of Civil Procedure, without obtaining a court order.
call_of_question: Which of the following is a method of service that may be used as an alternative to personal service on an individual under r.16.03, without a court order?

options.A: Leaving a copy of the document taped to the front door of the defendant's residence.
options.B: Sending the document by email to the defendant's known email address.
options.C: Leaving a copy of the document at the defendant's place of residence with an adult person who is apparently resident there, and mailing another copy to the same address on the same or following day.
options.D: Sending the document by fax to the defendant's place of employment.

explanation: Under r.16.03(5) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), as an alternative to personal service, a document may be served on an individual by leaving a copy at the individual's place of residence with an adult person who is apparently a resident there, and by sending a copy of the document to the defendant's place of residence by ordinary mail or prepaid courier on the same day or the following day. This two-step process — leaving with an adult resident plus mailing — constitutes valid service without a court order.
why_A_wrong: Taping a document to the front door of the residence is not a recognized method of alternative service under r.16.03. Simply leaving the document on the property without delivery to a person does not constitute valid service.
why_B_wrong: Email service is not a method of alternative service for an originating process (such as a statement of claim) under r.16.03 without consent of the party being served or a court order.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 16.03(5) permits service by leaving the document with an adult resident at the defendant's place of residence, combined with mailing another copy to the same address.
why_D_wrong: Fax to the place of employment is not a recognized method of alternative service under r.16.03 for an individual without a court order.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Alternative to Personal Service — Methods Under r.16.03
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.03(5)

tested_concepts: alternative to personal service — r.16.03(5)
adult resident plus mailing
no court order required for r.16.03

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Substituted Service — When Permitted and Court Order Required
fact_pattern: A plaintiff has made numerous attempts to personally serve a defendant over a period of two months, including multiple visits to the defendant's last known address, inquiries with the defendant's former employer, and a search of online directories. The defendant cannot be located. The plaintiff wants to serve the defendant by publishing notice in a local newspaper.
call_of_question: What must the plaintiff do under r.16.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure before using a substituted method of service such as newspaper publication?

options.A: The plaintiff may proceed with newspaper publication immediately after two failed personal service attempts; no court order is required.
options.B: The plaintiff must obtain a court order under r.16.04 authorizing substituted service; the court will grant the order if it is satisfied that personal service is impractical and the substituted method is likely to come to the defendant's attention.
options.C: Newspaper publication is never a valid form of substituted service in Ontario civil proceedings.
options.D: The plaintiff must serve the defendant's last known family member and then advertise in a newspaper simultaneously, without requiring a court order.

explanation: Under r.16.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where it appears to the court that it is impractical to effect prompt personal service of an originating process, the court may make an order for substituted service. The court may authorize service by any method — including newspaper publication — and will do so where: (1) personal service is impractical, and (2) the proposed substituted method is likely to come to the attention of the person to be served. A court order is mandatory; the plaintiff cannot unilaterally proceed with substituted service.
why_A_wrong: A court order is mandatory before substituted service may be used under r.16.04. The plaintiff cannot proceed with an alternative method of service simply after a fixed number of failed attempts.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 16.04 requires a court order for substituted service, granted where personal service is impractical and the substituted method will likely reach the defendant.
why_C_wrong: Newspaper publication can be authorized as a form of substituted service under r.16.04, provided a court order is obtained. It is not categorically unavailable in Ontario civil proceedings.
why_D_wrong: Serving a family member without court authorization does not satisfy the requirements of r.16.04. Substituted service requires a court order specifying the method permitted.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Substituted Service — When Permitted and Court Order Required
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.04

tested_concepts: substituted service requires court order — r.16.04
personal service must be impractical
substituted method must likely reach defendant

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: A plaintiff is attempting to serve a defendant who has left Ontario and whose whereabouts are completely unknown. The plaintiff cannot identify any method of substituted service that is likely to come to the defendant's attention. The plaintiff brings a motion asking the court to dispense with service entirely.
call_of_question: Can a court dispense with service entirely under the Rules of Civil Procedure, and if so, what must the plaintiff demonstrate?

options.A: No — a court can never dispense with service; it can only order a form of substituted service.
options.B: Yes — under r.16.04, a court may dispense with service entirely where it is satisfied that service is not reasonably possible and that dispensing with service is appropriate in the circumstances.
options.C: Yes — but only if the plaintiff agrees to indemnify the defendant for any costs arising from the default judgment obtained without service.
options.D: Yes — a court may dispense with service automatically after 6 months of failed service attempts without any further motion.

explanation: Under r.16.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court has authority not only to order substituted service but also to dispense with service entirely in appropriate circumstances. Where the court is satisfied that service of the originating process is not reasonably possible because the defendant's whereabouts are unknown and no method of substituted service would likely reach the defendant, the court has the discretion to make an order dispensing with service. This is an exceptional order reserved for cases where all reasonable efforts to notify the defendant have failed.
why_A_wrong: Rule 16.04 expressly empowers the court to dispense with service entirely, not only to order a form of substituted service. This power exists for exceptional circumstances where service is not reasonably possible.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.16.04, the court may dispense with service entirely where service is not reasonably possible and dispensing is appropriate.
why_C_wrong: There is no requirement that the plaintiff indemnify the defendant as a condition of dispensing with service. The court's order is based on practicality and fairness to the plaintiff, not an indemnity arrangement.
why_D_wrong: Service cannot be automatically dispensed with after a period of time. A court order is required under r.16.04 to dispense with service, and the plaintiff must bring a motion to obtain such an order.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Substituted Service — Dispensing with Service
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.04

tested_concepts: court may dispense with service entirely under r.16.04
exceptional circumstances required
service not reasonably possible

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Proof of Service — Affidavit of Service Requirements
fact_pattern: After personally serving a statement of claim on an individual defendant, a process server is asked to prepare the necessary documentation to prove that service occurred. The plaintiff's lawyer explains that this document will be filed with the court.
call_of_question: Under r.16.09 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how is proof of service of a document established, and what must the proof contain?

options.A: Proof of service is established by a written acknowledgment signed by the defendant confirming receipt of the documents.
options.B: Proof of service is established by filing an affidavit of service, sworn by the person who effected service, stating the time, date, place, and manner of service and identifying the document served.
options.C: Proof of service is established by a certificate signed by the plaintiff's counsel confirming that service was effected.
options.D: Proof of service is established by filing the document itself with the court; no separate proof document is required.

explanation: Under r.16.09 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), proof of service of a document may be made by an affidavit of the person who effected service. The affidavit must set out: the name of the person served; the date, time, and place of service; and the manner in which service was effected. This sworn document provides the court with the evidentiary basis to confirm that service was properly made. An affidavit of service is filed with the court when default proceedings are pursued or when service is otherwise in issue.
why_A_wrong: A signed acknowledgment from the defendant is not the required method of proof under r.16.09. The process server's sworn affidavit of service is the required document.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 16.09 requires an affidavit of service sworn by the person who effected service, containing the time, date, place, and manner of service.
why_C_wrong: A certificate from plaintiff's counsel is not the method of proving service under r.16.09. The affidavit must be sworn by the process server who actually effected service, not by counsel.
why_D_wrong: Filing the served document itself does not constitute proof of service. A separate affidavit of service sworn by the process server is required under r.16.09.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Proof of Service — Affidavit of Service Requirements
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.09

tested_concepts: proof of service by affidavit — r.16.09
required contents of affidavit of service
sworn by person who effected service

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Service Outside Ontario — Leave and Without Leave Under r.17
fact_pattern: A plaintiff incorporated in Ontario sues a British Columbia corporation for breach of a contract that was negotiated and to be performed entirely in Ontario. The plaintiff wishes to serve the statement of claim on the defendant at its registered office in Vancouver without first obtaining leave of the court.
call_of_question: Under r.17.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, may a plaintiff serve an originating process outside Ontario without first obtaining leave of the court, and if so, in what circumstances?

options.A: No — service outside Ontario always requires prior leave of the court; a motion under r.17.03 must be brought before service may be attempted.
options.B: Yes — under r.17.02, a plaintiff may serve an originating process outside Ontario without leave where one of the prescribed circumstances applies, including where the contract was made in Ontario or is to be performed in Ontario.
options.C: Yes — a plaintiff may serve outside Ontario without leave in any commercial dispute without restrictions.
options.D: No — service outside Ontario is not recognized in Ontario proceedings; the plaintiff must domesticate the claim by serving an Ontario agent of the defendant.

explanation: Under r.17.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), an originating process or notice of a reference may be served outside Ontario without leave of the court in a variety of prescribed circumstances. These include where the contract in dispute was made in Ontario, is to be performed in Ontario, or was breached in Ontario; where the tort was committed in Ontario; where there is real and substantial property situated in Ontario; and several other grounds. Rule 17.03 provides for service outside Ontario with leave in circumstances not covered by r.17.02. In this scenario, the contract was made and to be performed in Ontario — a ground listed in r.17.02 — so no leave is required.
why_A_wrong: Rule 17.02 permits service outside Ontario without leave in specified circumstances, including where the contract was made or is to be performed in Ontario. Leave under r.17.03 is only needed where r.17.02 does not apply.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.17.02, service outside Ontario is permitted without leave where the contract was made in Ontario or is to be performed in Ontario, among other grounds.
why_C_wrong: Service outside Ontario without leave is only permitted in the specific circumstances listed in r.17.02, not in all commercial disputes generally. The grounds are enumerated and restrictive.
why_D_wrong: Service outside Ontario on a foreign defendant is expressly recognized in Ontario proceedings under r.17. There is no requirement to identify an Ontario agent of the defendant as a precondition.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Service Outside Ontario — Leave and Without Leave Under r.17
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.17.02; r.17.03

tested_concepts: service outside Ontario without leave — r.17.02
prescribed circumstances including contract made in Ontario
r.17.03 — service with leave when r.17.02 inapplicable

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Service Outside Ontario — Leave Required Under r.17.03
fact_pattern: A plaintiff in Ontario wishes to sue a German corporation for an alleged misrepresentation made entirely in Germany. The misrepresentation induced the plaintiff to enter into a contract in Germany. No other connection to Ontario is apparent. The plaintiff applies for leave to serve the defendant in Germany under r.17.03.
call_of_question: Under r.17.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what must a plaintiff demonstrate to obtain leave to serve an originating process outside Ontario?

options.A: The plaintiff need only show that the defendant is a foreign corporation against whom a judgment would be recognizable in Ontario.
options.B: The plaintiff must demonstrate that: (1) there is a good arguable case on the merits; (2) there is a real and substantial connection between Ontario and the subject matter of the action; and (3) Ontario is the appropriate forum for the dispute.
options.C: The plaintiff must show that service within Ontario is not reasonably possible and that the defendant can be located in the foreign jurisdiction.
options.D: The plaintiff must show the defendant has assets in Ontario against which any eventual judgment could be enforced.

explanation: Under r.17.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where a plaintiff seeks leave to serve outside Ontario in circumstances not covered by r.17.02, the court will grant leave where it is satisfied that: (1) the claim discloses a good arguable case; (2) there is a real and substantial connection between Ontario and the facts on which the proceeding is based; and (3) Ontario is the appropriate forum for the proceeding. The court must be satisfied as to jurisdiction and forum. In the scenario, the misrepresentation occurred in Germany and the contract was formed in Germany — the absence of any Ontario connection will make it very difficult to satisfy the real and substantial connection requirement.
why_A_wrong: Leave under r.17.03 is not granted simply because the defendant is a foreign corporation with a recognizable judgment. The plaintiff must establish a good arguable case, a real and substantial connection to Ontario, and that Ontario is the appropriate forum.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 17.03 requires: a good arguable case, a real and substantial connection to Ontario, and that Ontario is the appropriate forum.
why_C_wrong: The plaintiff need not show that service within Ontario is impossible before seeking service outside Ontario. The test under r.17.03 focuses on jurisdiction and appropriate forum, not the impracticability of domestic service.
why_D_wrong: The existence of Ontario assets is not a required element of the r.17.03 test. While assets may be relevant to enforceability, the leave test focuses on arguable case, real and substantial connection, and appropriate forum.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Service Outside Ontario — Leave Required Under r.17.03
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.17.03

tested_concepts: r.17.03 — three-part test for leave to serve outside Ontario
real and substantial connection to Ontario required
appropriate forum consideration

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Alternative Service on Corporations — Registered Mail Under r.16.03
fact_pattern: A plaintiff's process server has been unable to personally serve Northgate Dynamics Inc., an Ontario corporation, at its registered office. The office was closed each time the process server attended. The plaintiff's lawyer considers using an alternative to personal service on the corporation without obtaining a court order.
call_of_question: Under r.16.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what alternative to personal service is available for service on a corporation without a court order?

options.A: Sending the originating process by email to the corporation's general inquiry email address.
options.B: Serving the originating process on any employee of the corporation found anywhere in Ontario.
options.C: Sending a copy of the originating process by registered mail to the registered office of the corporation, which constitutes an alternative to personal service under r.16.03.
options.D: Filing the originating process with the registrar and requesting the registrar to notify the corporation by ordinary mail.

explanation: Under r.16.03(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), as an alternative to personal service, a document may be served on a corporation incorporated or registered under a statute of Ontario by sending a copy of the document by registered mail to the registered office of the corporation as shown in the records of the Ministry of Government and Consumer Services. Service is effective on the day the registered mail is delivered, or if not delivered, on the fifth day after mailing. This alternative does not require a court order and is specifically designed to address the difficulty of personally serving corporations at their registered office.
why_A_wrong: Email to a general inquiry address is not an authorized alternative to personal service under r.16.03. The rule specifies registered mail to the registered office as the alternative for corporations.
why_B_wrong: Serving any employee of the corporation wherever found does not satisfy r.16.03. Service on a corporation requires leaving documents with a qualifying person (officer, director, agent, or person in apparent control) under r.16.02, or by registered mail to the registered office under r.16.03.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 16.03(2) permits service on a corporation by registered mail to its registered office as an alternative to personal service, without a court order.
why_D_wrong: Filing the originating process with the registrar for notification is not an authorized method of service under the Rules. Service obligations rest with the plaintiff, not the registrar.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Alternative Service on Corporations — Registered Mail Under r.16.03
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.03(2)

tested_concepts: alternative service on corporation — r.16.03(2)
registered mail to registered office
no court order required
